text: The surgical field shows advanced liver resection with exposure of the inferior vena cava, indicating progress in the procedure.

The caudate lobe is visibly detached from the inferior vena cava at the bottom of the surgical field. The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to dissect the liver parenchyma, proceeding with the transaction step by step.

The surgeon encounters bleeding from a torn short hepatic vein at its origin from the inferior vena cava. Compression is applied using the third arm to control the bleeding, while the left lobe of the liver is compressed due to incomplete exposure of the cava.

The surgical field shows clear landmarks, including the vena cava at the bottom and the falciform ligament. The left hepatic vein is positioned, and there is visible bleeding from the vena cava, which is not fully visible until complete removal.

The surgeon completes the division of the left lobe of the liver, revealing a small tear on the vena cava at the bottom of the surgical field. The assistant uses suction to clear the area, while the surgeon places a suture to address the tear.

The vena cava is fully exposed following the left hepatectomy extended to a portion of segment eight. A selective suture is placed on the vena cava, effectively controlling the bleeding. The surgeon prepares for bile duct reconstruction by bringing a long jejunal loop up to the liver, setting up for a Roux-en-Y reconstruction.
